# Roman Kocherezhchenko

> AI-Enabled Engineer. I design, build, and ship software — fast static sites, Telegram bots, edge deploys — and write deep technical notes on the systems work behind them. Contact: https://t.me/roman_kocherezhchenko

The site is bilingual (English and Ukrainian); the English pages below are canonical. The flagship writing is a series on **risp**, a small, zero-dependency Lisp interpreter written in Rust (a tree-walker, a bytecode VM, and an optional Cranelift JIT) — not the 2019 "Risp (in (Rust) (Lisp))" tutorial of the same name — built by pairing with Claude Code.

## risp — a Lisp interpreter in Rust

- [risp source on GitHub](https://github.com/kissishka/risp): The full Rust source for risp — the tree-walker, bytecode VM, and Cranelift JIT described in the posts below, including the benchmark suite and the `agree()` differential tests.
- [Building a Lisp interpreter in Rust with Claude Code](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/blog/building-a-lisp-in-rust-with-claude-code/): The overview — one `Value` enum as both AST and runtime value, three execution engines sharing one evaluator, a no-panic error contract, and the differential-testing discipline (`agree()`) that keeps AI-written systems code correct against a reference interpreter.
- [Designing a Lisp interpreter in Rust with no stack overflows](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/blog/no-stack-overflow-lisp-interpreter-rust/): How an explicit-stack CEK evaluator, a hand-written iterative `Drop`, and loop-based printing/equality let the interpreter read, free, compare, and evaluate million-deep structures without overflowing the Rust call stack.
- [How a bytecode VM in Rust beat CPython 3.14](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/blog/bytecode-vm-faster-than-cpython/): Eleven profile-guided optimization steps — lexical-addressed locals, unboxed `i64` arithmetic, an unchecked hot loop, in-place self-tail-calls — taking `fib(30)` from 1682 ms to 95 ms and beating CPython on loops and arithmetic.
- [Adding a Cranelift JIT that runs 10–23× faster than CPython](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/blog/cranelift-jit-for-a-lisp-in-rust/): An opt-in Cranelift JIT for the integer subset — a decline-don't-guess type checker, lowering `if`/`cond`/`let` to Cranelift IR, inline signed-overflow detection, tail-calls as loop back-edges, and a deopt guard instead of a crash.
- [Macros and quasiquote: code that writes code in risp](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/blog/lisp-macros-quasiquote-rust/): How `defmacro`, demand-driven expansion, the quasiquote engine, and `gensym` let a Lisp grow control structures like `while` and `unless` that no function can express.
- [Writing a Lisp reader in Rust: from text to a Value tree](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/blog/writing-a-lisp-reader-in-rust/): The reader — a Lisp-agnostic tokenizer, an iterative explicit-stack parser that can't overflow on deep nesting, reader-macro desugaring (`'x` → `(quote x)`), dotted-pair handling, and an atom classifier that declines ambiguous numbers instead of guessing.
- [Lexical scope in a Rust Lisp: environments, closures, and a two-path drop](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/blog/lexical-scope-in-a-rust-lisp/): The scope model — an `Rc<RefCell<Environment>>` chain, iterative lookup and `set!`, closures that capture frames by reference (`child(&c.env)`), and a fast/slow `Drop` that frees a million-deep scope chain without recursing or allocating on the hot path.
- [Why risp's standard library is written in risp, not Rust](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/blog/risp-standard-library-in-lisp-not-rust/): The native/Lisp boundary — a flat table of std-only builtins, an int-preserving numeric tower, and why `map`/`filter`/`fold` live in a risp prelude: a native higher-order builtin would re-enter the evaluator on the host stack and could overflow.
- [Why a native-compiling Lisp loses to CPython on `map`](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/blog/why-cpython-beats-a-native-compiling-lisp/): risp's JIT beats CPython 3.14 by 9–40× on integer benchmarks yet loses 3× on `map`+`fold` — because the bottleneck is ~400,000 `Rc<Pair>` cons-cell allocations, not interpreter dispatch, so the integer JIT correctly emits no machine code for boxed-value list traversal.
- [Fusing map, filter, and fold so a Lisp pipeline allocates once](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/blog/fusing-map-filter-fold-into-one-pass/): Transducers drive intermediate allocation from O(n) to O(1) but still lose on the per-element closure call; a monomorphizing `sum-of` macro lowers a `map`/`filter` pipeline to a native `i64` loop the existing JIT compiles — 4 ms vs CPython's 24 ms.
- [Writing an HTTP/1.1 client and server in a zero-dependency Lisp](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/blog/http-server-in-a-zero-dependency-lisp/): Two new `Value` variants (a `Bytes` buffer and an opaque TCP socket) and about twenty `std::net` socket, bytes, and string builtins — with every byte of HTTP logic written in risp itself, so a default `cargo build` still resolves zero crates.
- [Production-hardening a hobby Lisp's HTTP stack](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/blog/hardening-a-hobby-interpreters-http-stack/): TLS gated behind a `--features tls` Cargo flag (rustls), `try`/`catch` as a 30-line builtin, and a quadratic byte-buffer read loop replaced by a linear Rust primitive.
- [Learning Rust by reading a real Lisp interpreter](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/blog/learn-rust-by-reading-a-lisp-interpreter/): The repo's `learn/` directory turns the working codebase into a Rust course — an easy-to-hard file reading order from `error.rs` to `eval.rs`, plus a set of concept lessons each anchored to a real file-and-line pointer.

## Writing on GEO & SEO — optimizing this site for AI search

- [Structured data that makes a blog AI-quotable](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/blog/structured-data-that-makes-a-blog-ai-quotable/): The JSON-LD this site serves — a shared Person + WebSite graph with sameAs anchors, and a per-post BlogPosting carrying FAQPage, speakable, and isBasedOn.
- [Writing so an AI will cite you](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/blog/writing-so-an-ai-will-cite-you/): The mechanical properties of a citable passage — an answer-first lede, a named subject in every sentence, an attached number, and each claim tied to inspectable source.
- [llms.txt, robots, and the HTML that AI crawlers actually read](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/blog/llms-txt-and-html-ai-crawlers-read/): Serving the whole page in one fetch — near-zero client JS, an open robots.txt, correct hreflang, per-post sitemap lastmod, and this llms.txt.
- [The on-page ceiling: GEO is mostly off your site](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/blog/the-on-page-ceiling-geo-is-mostly-off-your-site/): Why a 93 technical and 86 structured-data score still nets 59 overall — an entity name collision, thin off-site authority, and the work that isn't code.

## About

- [Home](https://roman-kocherezhchenko.com/en/): Who I am and how to get in touch.
